column1: fact
A regulation baseball has exactly 108 hand-stitched double stitches.
Table tennis balls travel off the paddle at speeds up to 70 mph, making it one of the fastest racket sports relative to playing distance.
The Olympic gold medal is actually 92.5% silver, coated with at least 6 grams of gold.
Wilt Chamberlain scored 100 points in a single NBA game in 1962 — and there is no known video footage of the complete game.
Golf balls have between 300 and 500 dimples, and the pattern directly affects flight distance and trajectory.
The Tour de France is so grueling that riders consume roughly 8,000 calories per day during the race.
In cricket, a ball that bounces twice before reaching the batter is called a no-ball, and the batting team gets a free run.
Usain Bolt's top speed during his 9.58-second 100m world record was 27.8 mph — faster than the speed limit in most school zones.
A soccer ball is traditionally made of 32 panels — 12 pentagons and 20 hexagons — mimicking a truncated icosahedron.
The Stanley Cup has its own bodyguard who accompanies it everywhere it travels.
During the 1904 Olympics in St. Louis, the marathon winner was disqualified because he rode in a car for 11 miles of the race.
Fencing is one of only five sports that have been featured at every modern Olympic Games since 1896.
The fastest recorded tennis serve is 163.7 mph, hit by Sam Groth in 2012.
An NBA basketball court's three-point line is 23 feet 9 inches from the basket at the top of the arc.
Rugby was allegedly invented when William Webb Ellis picked up the ball during a football match and ran with it in 1823.
The longest professional baseball game lasted 33 innings and took over 8 hours to complete.
Formula 1 drivers lose about 6-8 pounds of body weight during a single race due to the extreme heat in the cockpit.
A badminton shuttlecock can leave the racket at over 200 mph, making it the fastest initial projectile speed in any racket sport.
The 1950 FIFA World Cup final in Brazil drew an estimated 199,854 spectators to the Maracanã — the largest crowd ever at a football match.
Michael Phelps has won 23 Olympic gold medals — more than many entire countries have won in their Olympic history.
Sumo wrestlers follow a strict diet called chankonabe, a protein-rich stew, and consume up to 7,000 calories a day.
The yellow jersey (maillot jaune) in the Tour de France was introduced in 1919 so spectators could easily spot the race leader.
Korfball, invented in the Netherlands in 1902, is one of the few mixed-gender team sports played competitively at the international level.
A regulation NFL football is inflated to between 12.5 and 13.5 psi and must weigh between 14 and 15 ounces.
The fastest goal in FIFA World Cup history was scored just 10.89 seconds after kickoff by Hakan Şükür of Turkey in 2002.
Bo Jackson was named an All-Star in both Major League Baseball and the NFL — the only athlete to achieve this in two major American sports.
The ancient Olympic Games included a combat sport called pankration, which combined boxing and wrestling with almost no rules.
An ice hockey puck can travel at over 100 mph when slapped by a professional player.
Ethiopian runner Abebe Bikila won the 1960 Olympic marathon running barefoot through the streets of Rome.
Lacrosse is one of the oldest team sports in North America, with Indigenous peoples playing it for centuries before European contact.
A tennis ball loses about 1 inch of bounce height after just one hour of play due to internal pressure loss.
The Ryder Cup in golf has been contested between Europe and the United States since 1927.
Pelé scored 1,283 goals in his career — a record recognized by the Guinness World Records.
In Australian Rules Football, the playing field is an oval that can be up to 185 meters long, making it one of the largest in any sport.
The first basketball game used a peach basket as the goal, and someone had to climb a ladder to retrieve the ball after each score.
A professional cyclist's legs can produce about 400 watts of sustained power during a mountain stage.
The Olympic flame has been carried by relay runners since the 1936 Berlin Games.
Sachin Tendulkar scored 100 international centuries across Test and One Day International cricket — a record that may stand for decades.
Handball is the second most popular team sport in Europe after soccer, with professional leagues in Germany, France, and Spain.
The Marathon des Sables is a 156-mile ultramarathon across the Sahara Desert, and competitors must carry their own food and supplies.
Wayne Gretzky holds or shares 61 NHL records, including most career goals (894) and most career assists (1,963).
In polo, the playing field is roughly nine acres — the largest field of any organized sport.
The 1992 U.S. Olympic basketball 'Dream Team' won their games by an average margin of 43.8 points.
Table tennis became an Olympic sport in 1988, and China has dominated the event ever since.
A baseball pitcher's arm rotates at up to 7,500 degrees per second during a fastball — the fastest recorded human motion.
The FIFA World Cup trophy is made of 18-karat gold and weighs approximately 13.6 pounds.
Jai alai pelota (ball) can reach speeds of 188 mph, making it one of the fastest-moving balls in any sport.
The first modern Olympic Games in Athens 1896 featured just 241 athletes from 14 nations competing in 43 events.
Roger Federer and Rafael Nadal have combined for over 40 Grand Slam singles titles.
Curling stones are made from a specific type of granite found on Ailsa Craig, a small island off the coast of Scotland.
In water polo, players tread water for the entire game — roughly 30 minutes of active play — and can swim up to 3 miles per match.
The decathlon requires athletes to compete in 10 track-and-field events over two consecutive days.
Brazil is the only country to have appeared in every FIFA World Cup since the tournament's inception in 1930.
Sir Don Bradman's Test cricket batting average of 99.94 is considered the greatest statistical achievement in any major sport.
Speed skaters can reach speeds of over 35 mph on Olympic-size oval tracks.
The Haka, a traditional Māori war dance, is performed by New Zealand's All Blacks rugby team before every match.
Kipchoge Keino, a Kenyan runner, overcame a gallbladder infection to win the 1,500m gold at the 1968 Olympics.
A regulation volleyball court is 18 meters long and 9 meters wide, with the net set at 2.43 meters for men.
The Indianapolis 500 has been held annually since 1911, making it one of the oldest and most prestigious auto races in the world.
Gymnast Nadia Comăneci scored the first perfect 10.0 in Olympic gymnastics history at the 1976 Montreal Games — she was 14 years old.
The NBA's shot clock is 24 seconds, inspired by Danny Biasone, who divided 48 minutes by the average number of shots in a game.
In kabaddi, a raider must hold a single breath while chanting 'kabaddi' repeatedly during their attack run.
The longest recorded rally in professional tennis lasted 29 minutes and consisted of 643 shots.
Field hockey is played in over 100 countries and is the third most popular sport in the world by participation.
An Olympic swimming pool holds approximately 660,000 gallons of water.
The Boston Marathon has been held annually since 1897, making it the world's oldest annual marathon.
Lionel Messi has won the Ballon d'Or award eight times — more than any other player in football history.
In competitive archery, the bullseye (X ring) on a target at 70 meters is about the size of a grapefruit.
The 1972 Miami Dolphins remain the only NFL team to complete an entire season undefeated, going 17-0.
Cricket matches can last up to five days in the Test format, and still end in a draw.
Alpine ski racers experience forces of up to 3 Gs in turns — comparable to what fighter pilots experience.
The longest hole-in-one ever recorded in golf measured 517 yards, achieved by Robert Mitera in 1965.
Kenyan and Ethiopian runners have dominated long-distance running, winning the majority of Olympic and World Championship medals since the 1960s.
A professional squash player can run the equivalent of 2.5 miles during a single match on a court just 32 feet long.
The FIFA Women's World Cup was first held in 1991 in China, with the United States winning the inaugural tournament.
Wladimir Klitschko holds a PhD in sports science — earning him the nickname 'Dr. Steelhammer.'
In biathlon, athletes must ski cross-country and then immediately steady themselves to shoot at targets 50 meters away.
The Triple Crown in horse racing requires winning the Kentucky Derby, Preakness Stakes, and Belmont Stakes in a single season.
Michael Jordan was cut from his high school varsity basketball team as a sophomore before becoming the greatest player of his era.
A Major League Baseball umpire makes roughly 280 ball-or-strike calls per game.
Sepak takraw, a sport popular in Southeast Asia, is essentially volleyball played with the feet using a rattan ball.
The Olympic torch relay for the 2004 Athens Games covered 78,000 kilometers across every continent.
Serena Williams has won 23 Grand Slam singles titles — the most in the Open Era.
In competitive rowing, an eight-person crew can move their boat at speeds over 14 mph.
The first rugby World Cup was held in 1987 and was won by New Zealand.
Fencer Valentina Vezzali of Italy won six Olympic medals across five consecutive Games.
Professional darts players stand 7 feet 9.25 inches from the dartboard and must hit a target area smaller than a postage stamp to score 180.
The Mongol Derby, at 1,000 kilometers across the Mongolian steppe, is the longest horse race in the world.
LeBron James became the NBA's all-time leading scorer, surpassing Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's record of 38,387 career points.
In competitive weightlifting, athletes can lift more than three times their own body weight in the clean and jerk.
The Tour de France covers approximately 3,500 kilometers over 21 stages across 23 days.
Base jumping is so dangerous that it has an estimated fatality rate of 1 in 2,317 jumps.
Simone Biles has four gymnastics elements named after her — skills so difficult that she was the first to perform them in competition.
The dead ball era in baseball (1900–1919) saw entire seasons where league batting averages dipped below .250.
In Japanese sumo, if a wrestler's topknot (chonmage) is cut during a match, the offending wrestler is disqualified.
Soccer's offside rule was first introduced in 1863 and has been modified multiple times since.
The fastest recorded bowling ball speed in professional tenpin bowling is over 27 mph.
Babe Ruth hit 714 career home runs while also maintaining a career pitching ERA of 2.28.
The sport of skeleton involves racing headfirst down an ice track at speeds exceeding 80 mph on a tiny sled.
In professional boxing, the ring is actually a square — the term 'ring' dates back to when spectators formed a circle around fighters.
Jim Thorpe, a Native American athlete, won gold in both the decathlon and pentathlon at the 1912 Olympics.
The record for the longest point in a professional tennis match lasted over 29 minutes during a 1984 Virginia Slims event.
Valentino Rossi won nine MotoGP World Championships across three different engine classifications.
In hurling, one of Ireland's national sports, the ball (sliotar) can travel at speeds over 100 mph when struck with the hurley.
The Paralympic Games have grown from 400 athletes in 1960 to over 4,400 athletes competing in recent editions.
A regulation basketball hoop is exactly 10 feet from the floor — a height that has remained unchanged since James Naismith invented the game in 1891.
The longest match in tennis history lasted 11 hours and 5 minutes: Isner vs. Mahut at Wimbledon in 2010.
Eddie 'The Eagle' Edwards became Britain's first Olympic ski jumper in 1988 despite finishing last in both events.
In Australian Rules Football, a player who kicks the ball between the two tall goalposts scores 6 points.
Ayrton Senna won 41 Formula 1 races and is widely considered one of the greatest drivers in the sport's history.
The NBA's tallest player, Gheorghe Mureșan, stood 7 feet 7 inches tall.
In the sport of Kabaddi, teams of seven compete on a court roughly the size of a badminton court.
Chess boxing alternates between rounds of chess and boxing — a player can win by checkmate or knockout.
The Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race covers 938 miles across Alaska and takes the winning team about 8-9 days to complete.
Carl Lewis won nine Olympic gold medals across four consecutive Olympics in track and field.
In rugby union, a converted try is worth 7 points — 5 for the try and 2 for the conversion kick.
The record for the most goals scored in a single FIFA World Cup tournament belongs to Just Fontaine, who scored 13 in 1958.
An Olympic-level gymnast can experience forces of up to 14 times their body weight during a dismount landing.
The Daytona 500 is often called 'The Great American Race' and has been held annually since 1959.
Manny Pacquiao is the only boxer in history to win world titles in eight different weight divisions.
In competitive sailing, boats in the America's Cup can reach speeds exceeding 50 mph on hydrofoils.
The world record for holding one's breath underwater is over 24 minutes, set by Budimir Šobat.
Wilt Chamberlain once averaged 48.5 minutes per game over an entire NBA season — more than the 48-minute game length — due to overtime games.
The Highland Games in Scotland include events like caber tossing, where athletes flip a 19-foot wooden pole end over end.
In synchronized swimming (now called artistic swimming), athletes can hold their breath for up to three minutes during routines.
Jackie Robinson broke Major League Baseball's color barrier in 1947, wearing number 42 for the Brooklyn Dodgers — now retired across all MLB teams.
An F1 car's brakes can reach temperatures of over 1,000°C during heavy braking zones.
The first Winter Olympics were held in Chamonix, France, in 1924, featuring 258 athletes from 16 nations.
In professional cricket, the fastest recorded bowling speed is 161.3 km/h (100.2 mph), delivered by Shoaib Akhtar of Pakistan.
The longest field goal in NFL history is 66 yards, kicked by Justin Tucker in 2021.
Freestyle wrestling has been part of the Olympic Games since the very first modern Olympics in 1896.
In beach volleyball, teams consist of just two players per side covering a full-size sand court.
Eliud Kipchoge ran a marathon in under two hours (1:59:40) during the INEOS 1:59 Challenge in 2019.
The FIFA World Cup trophy must be returned to FIFA after each tournament — winning countries receive a gold-plated replica.
In competitive rock climbing, speed climbers can scale a 15-meter wall in under 5 seconds.
Pelé is the youngest player to score in a World Cup final — he was 17 years old when he scored twice in the 1958 final.
Motor racing's 24 Hours of Le Mans has been held annually since 1923, with teams driving continuously through day and night.
In water polo, a player who commits a major foul is excluded for 20 seconds — and their team plays shorthanded.
Jesse Owens won four gold medals at the 1936 Berlin Olympics in front of Adolf Hitler.
The longest recorded snooker frame lasted over 2 hours and featured extended safety exchanges.
In professional cycling, domestiques are teammates who sacrifice their own chances to help the team leader win.
The Harlem Globetrotters have won over 26,000 games and lost fewer than 400 in their exhibition history.
A regulation FIFA soccer ball must weigh between 410 and 450 grams and be inflated to 0.6–1.1 atmospheres.
In sumo wrestling, there are 82 winning techniques (kimarite) officially recognized by the Japan Sumo Association.
The highest-scoring game in NBA history ended 186-184 in triple overtime between Detroit and Denver in 1983.
In bobsled, four-man teams can reach speeds exceeding 95 mph on an icy track.
Zinedine Zidane scored two headers in the 1998 World Cup final to lead France to their first World Cup victory.
The longest winning streak in professional sports belongs to the 1972-74 UCLA Bruins men's basketball team at 88 consecutive games.
An Olympic fencing bout can be decided by a single touch worth one point.
The first known golf course was the Old Links at Musselburgh in Scotland, which dates back to 1672.
In competitive figure skating, a quadruple jump requires the skater to complete four full rotations in the air.
Red Bull Stratos: Felix Baumgartner skydived from 128,100 feet, breaking the sound barrier with his body at Mach 1.25.
The Super Bowl halftime show regularly attracts more viewers than the game itself.
In professional darts, Phil Taylor won 16 World Championship titles during his career.
A hockey goalie's pads can weigh up to 50 pounds combined.
Neymar's transfer from Barcelona to Paris Saint-Germain in 2017 cost €222 million — the most expensive transfer in football history.
The original rules of basketball written by James Naismith in 1891 contained just 13 rules.
In competitive rowing, the cox (coxswain) steers the boat and calls the stroke rate — they're typically the lightest person on the team.
The first recorded game of cricket took place in the 16th century in southeast England.
An Olympic diver enters the water from a 10-meter platform at approximately 35 mph.
The Vince Lombardi Trophy, awarded to the Super Bowl winner, is handcrafted by Tiffany & Co. from sterling silver.
In the sport of curling, sweeping the ice in front of the stone can increase its travel distance by up to 15 feet.
Diego Maradona's 'Hand of God' goal in the 1986 World Cup was followed minutes later by what FIFA voters named the 'Goal of the Century.'
The NBA draft pick DeAndre Jordan was originally recruited to play football in college.
A Formula 1 pit stop can be completed in under two seconds, with a crew of over 20 mechanics.
In archery, Olympic recurve bows can launch arrows at over 150 mph.
The marathon distance of 26.2 miles was standardized at the 1908 London Olympics to finish in front of the royal box.
Cristiano Ronaldo has scored over 900 career goals across club and international football.
In ski jumping, athletes can fly over 250 meters (820 feet) through the air on the largest hills.
The All Blacks of New Zealand have a winning percentage of roughly 77% across all Test matches in rugby history.
In competitive swimming, the difference between gold and silver is often measured in hundredths of a second.
The tallest athlete to compete in the Olympics was basketball player Sun Mingming, who stands 7 feet 9 inches tall.
Tiger Woods held the number one world golf ranking for a record total of 683 weeks.
In the decathlon, athletes earn points based on performance in each event — a perfect score would exceed 12,000 points.
The oldest known sport with a continuous competitive tradition is wrestling, with evidence dating back over 15,000 years in cave paintings.
In professional rugby league, each team is allowed a limited number of interchanges (substitutions) per match.
Secretariat's 1973 Belmont Stakes record of 2:24 for 1.5 miles still stands — he won by 31 lengths.
The world's highest golf course, La Paz Golf Club in Bolivia, sits at an elevation of over 10,800 feet.
In competitive table tennis, players can put up to 9,000 RPM of spin on the ball.
The longest UFC fight went five rounds (25 minutes), but early UFC events had matches with virtually no time limit.
Lewis Hamilton and Michael Schumacher share the record of seven Formula 1 World Drivers' Championships.
In volleyball, the libero is a defensive specialist who wears a different colored jersey and cannot attack the ball above net height.
The first intercollegiate football game in America was played between Rutgers and Princeton in 1869.
Kipchoge's official marathon world record of 2:01:09, set at the 2022 Berlin Marathon, averages roughly 4:37 per mile.
In Olympic weightlifting, the barbell spins on specialized bearings so lifters can rotate underneath it during the clean.
A professional golfer's swing generates clubhead speeds exceeding 120 mph.
The Copa América is the oldest international continental football competition, first held in 1916.
In the pole vault, athletes launch themselves over bars set higher than 6 meters (nearly 20 feet) using a fiberglass pole.
The NBA's Boston Celtics have won 17 championships — tied with the Los Angeles Lakers for the most in league history.
In test cricket, a batsman can be out in ten different ways, including the rare 'handled the ball' and 'obstructing the field.'
The average lifespan of a baseball in a Major League game is just 6 pitches before it's replaced.
A competitive sprint kayaker can paddle at speeds over 12 mph using just upper body strength.
The FIFA Club World Cup features champion clubs from every continental confederation competing for a global title.
In competitive gymnastics, the vault takes less than 5 seconds from run to landing.
The longest hole in competitive golf is the par-7 seventh hole at Satsuki Golf Club in Japan, measuring 964 yards.
Wayne Rooney became England's all-time leading scorer with 53 goals in international football.
In skeleton, athletes steer their sled at 80+ mph using only subtle shifts in body weight and shoulder pressure.
The record for consecutive NFL starts is held by Brett Favre with 297 regular-season games.
In competitive surfing, waves are scored on a scale of 0–10 based on commitment, degree of difficulty, and speed.
The first known female professional athlete was Hélène de Pourtalès, who won a gold medal in sailing at the 1900 Paris Olympics.
In rugby sevens, each half lasts only seven minutes, making it one of the fastest team sports.
A Major League Baseball pitcher throws the ball from a mound that is exactly 60 feet 6 inches from home plate.
In speedway motorcycle racing, the bikes have no brakes — riders control speed using the throttle and controlled slides.
The record for the most Olympic medals won by a single athlete belongs to Michael Phelps with 28 total.
In field hockey, the ball can only be struck with the flat side of the stick.
The 1966 FIFA World Cup final featured a controversial 'ghost goal' when Geoff Hurst's shot hit the crossbar and bounced on or near the line.
Shaquille O'Neal made only one three-point shot in his entire 19-year NBA career.
In competitive climbing, bouldering problems are completed without ropes on walls no higher than about 4.5 meters.
The Rás Tailteann (Tour of Ireland) is one of the oldest stage cycling races in Europe.
A regulation tennis court is 78 feet long, and the net stands 3 feet high at the center.
In gaelic football, players can carry the ball for four steps before they must bounce or solo it off their foot.
Novak Djokovic has won 24 Grand Slam singles titles — the most in men's tennis history.
The 1980 'Miracle on Ice' saw the U.S. amateur hockey team defeat the heavily favored Soviet Union 4-3 at the Lake Placid Olympics.
In luge, athletes lie on their backs and steer a sled at speeds exceeding 90 mph using only calf pressure on the runners.
The record for the most touchdowns in a single NFL season is 31, set by LaDainian Tomlinson in 2006.
A shuttlecock used in badminton is made from 16 goose feathers arranged in a cone shape.
In the Tour de France, the polka-dot jersey is awarded to the best mountain climber.
The 1994 baseball strike canceled the World Series for the first time since 1904.
In handball, goalkeepers are the only players allowed inside the 6-meter zone around the goal.
Usain Bolt won gold in the 100m and 200m at three consecutive Olympics (2008, 2012, 2016) before his 4x100m relay medal from 2008 was stripped.
The world's largest stadium by capacity is the Narendra Modi Stadium in Ahmedabad, India, which holds 132,000 spectators.
In competitive speed skating, athletes lean so far into turns that their hands nearly touch the ice.
A bowling pin must fall by 10 degrees or more to be counted as knocked down.
In professional snooker, a maximum break of 147 requires potting 15 reds, 15 blacks, and all six colors in sequence.
The New Zealand All Blacks have performed the Haka before matches since 1884.
In rhythmic gymnastics, athletes perform routines with five apparatus: rope, hoop, ball, clubs, and ribbon.
The fastest serve in women's tennis was 136.7 mph, hit by Georgina García Pérez in 2018.
In the biathlon, athletes shoot at targets just 45mm in diameter from 50 meters away while standing.
The Ashes, cricket's most famous rivalry series between England and Australia, has been contested since 1882.
In short track speed skating, the entire race distance of 1,000 meters takes place on a 111.12-meter oval.
Don Bradman's final Test innings required just 4 runs for a career average of 100 — he was bowled for a duck.
In professional cycling, the peloton can travel at average speeds exceeding 25 mph for hours.
The world record for consecutive free throws made is 5,221, set by Ted St. Martin.
In competitive trampoline, athletes bounce over 8 meters (26 feet) high during their routines.
Cathy Freeman lit the Olympic cauldron and won the 400m gold at the 2000 Sydney Olympics.
In water polo, the shot clock gives teams just 30 seconds to take a shot.
The heaviest sumo wrestler on record, Konishiki, weighed approximately 633 pounds during his career.
In competitive luge, athletes begin by rocking and paddling the ice with spiked gloves to build momentum.
The Boston Red Sox went 86 years between World Series championships, from 1918 to 2004.
In Olympic fencing, electronic scoring systems detect touches that last as briefly as 1/25th of a second.
The average NFL career lasts just 3.3 years.
Pelota, a traditional Basque ball game, is played on a court called a fronton and has been a cultural tradition for centuries.
In competitive rowing, an eight-oared shell can cover 2,000 meters in under 5 minutes and 30 seconds.
Wayne Gretzky had so many assists that even with zero goals, he'd still be the NHL's all-time leading scorer.
In the high jump, the Fosbury Flop technique — jumping backward over the bar — was popularized at the 1968 Olympics.
The sport of triathlon combines swimming, cycling, and running, with the Ironman distance totaling 140.6 miles.
In competitive taekwondo, electronic scoring vests register the impact of kicks to the body in real time.
Marta Vieira da Silva, known as Marta, is the all-time leading scorer in FIFA Women's World Cup history.
A Formula 1 steering wheel contains over 20 buttons and switches, and can cost upward of $50,000.
In water polo, field players can only touch the ball with one hand at a time.
The Green Bay Packers are the only community-owned franchise in American professional sports.
In the hammer throw, athletes spin inside a 7-foot circle and launch a 16-pound ball attached to a wire over 80 meters.
The record for the fastest red card in professional soccer is two seconds after kickoff.
In competitive diving, judges score each dive on a scale from 0 to 10 and multiply by a difficulty rating.
The Duckworth-Lewis-Stern method is a mathematical formula used to calculate target scores in rain-affected cricket matches.
An Olympic-level javelin thrower can launch the spear over 90 meters (nearly 300 feet).
In professional basketball, Muggsy Bogues played in the NBA at just 5 feet 3 inches tall.
The longest penalty shootout in recorded football history went to 48 penalties in a 2005 Namibian cup match.
In skeleton, the sled weighs between 29 and 43 kilograms and has no steering or braking mechanism.
The world record for the 100-meter backstroke in swimming has been broken over 30 times since electronic timing began.
In orienteering, athletes use a map and compass to navigate between checkpoints in unfamiliar terrain as fast as possible.
The first Super Bowl in 1967 was broadcast simultaneously by CBS and NBC, and tickets cost just $12.
In Olympic modern pentathlon, athletes compete in fencing, swimming, show jumping, and a combined run-shoot event — all in one day.
The cricket bat's maximum width is 4.25 inches, and it can weigh up to 3 pounds.
In professional MMA, the octagon cage is 30 feet in diameter and surrounded by a 6-foot-high fence.
The fastest recorded soccer kick reached 131 mph.
In competitive canoe slalom, athletes navigate through 18-25 gates on whitewater rapids while being timed to the hundredth of a second.
The longest winning streak in women's professional tennis belongs to Martina Navratilova at 74 consecutive matches.
In ice hockey, the puck is frozen before games to reduce bouncing on the ice surface.
A standard Olympic track is 400 meters around, and the lanes are 1.22 meters wide.
In competitive show jumping, horses and riders must clear obstacles up to 1.65 meters (5 feet 5 inches) high.
The Golden State Warriors won 73 regular-season games in 2015-16 — the most in NBA history.
In rugby union, a scrum involves eight players from each team binding together and pushing against each other.
A cricket pitch is exactly 22 yards long, which is also the length of one chain — an old English unit of measurement.
In the NBA, the free throw line is 15 feet from the backboard.
Geraint Thomas, a former track cyclist, won the Tour de France in 2018, showcasing the crossover potential between cycling disciplines.
In competitive archery, archers at the Olympic level can group arrows within a 5-centimeter circle at 70 meters.
The World Chess Boxing Organisation sanctions fights that alternate between 3-minute chess rounds and 3-minute boxing rounds.
Alexander Ovechkin holds the record for most power-play goals in NHL history.
In the sport of polo, a chukker (period of play) lasts 7 minutes, and a full match consists of 4 to 8 chukkers.
A volleyball spike from a professional player can travel at over 80 mph.
The first known written rules of soccer (association football) were established in 1863 by the newly formed Football Association in England.
In professional boxing, a championship fight consists of 12 three-minute rounds.
Haile Gebrselassie of Ethiopia set 27 distance-running world records during his career.
In ski cross, four athletes race simultaneously down a course filled with jumps, rollers, and banked turns.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar scored 38,387 career points over 20 NBA seasons using his signature skyhook.
In badminton doubles, the court is wider but serves must still land in the shorter service box.
The world record for the most consecutive push-ups is 10,507, set by Minoru Yoshida in 1980.
The oldest trophy in international sport is the America's Cup in sailing, first awarded in 1851.
In competitive rowing, a coxless pair requires two athletes to row in perfect synchronization without a coxswain to steer.
The record for the most goals in a single World Cup match is 5, achieved by Oleg Salenko of Russia in 1994.
In shot put, the men's shot weighs 7.26 kilograms (16 pounds) and must be 'put' from the shoulder with one hand.
Katie Ledecky has won seven Olympic gold medals in swimming and holds multiple world records in freestyle distance events.
The Boston Celtics once had an eight-year dynasty from 1959 to 1966, winning the NBA championship every single season.
In beach volleyball, hand signals are used behind players' backs to communicate blocking strategy to their partner.
A NASCAR stock car can reach speeds over 200 mph on superspeedways like Daytona and Talladega.
In competitive cliff diving, athletes leap from platforms up to 27 meters (89 feet) high.
The record for the most Grand Slam titles in women's tennis belongs to Margaret Court with 24.
In freestyle skiing moguls, athletes navigate a field of bumps while performing two aerial jumps — all while being timed.
The world record in the men's long jump, 8.95 meters (29 feet 4.5 inches), was set by Mike Powell in 1991.
In professional cycling, a breakaway rider may spend hours alone at the front of the race, riding into headwinds without drafting.
The FIFA Confederations Cup was held eight times between 1992 and 2017 before being discontinued.
In fencing, the three weapons — foil, épée, and sabre — each have different rules about valid target areas.
The first known woman to run the Boston Marathon was Kathrine Switzer in 1967 — a race official tried to physically remove her mid-race.
In speed climbing, athletes race up a standardized 15-meter wall with identical holds at every competition worldwide.
Bill Russell won 11 NBA championships in 13 seasons with the Boston Celtics.
In F1, DRS (Drag Reduction System) allows trailing cars to open their rear wing on certain straights to improve overtaking.
A professional baseball player has roughly 400 milliseconds to decide whether to swing at a 95 mph fastball.
In competitive dragon boat racing, teams of 20 paddlers synchronize their strokes to propel a 40-foot boat.
The FIFA Ballon d'Or was first awarded in 1956 to Stanley Matthews.
In Olympic shooting, pistol events require athletes to hit a bullseye just 11.5 mm in diameter from 25 meters.
The first African nation to reach a World Cup quarterfinal was Cameroon in 1990, led by 38-year-old Roger Milla.
In professional ice skating, a triple axel requires 3.5 rotations in the air — the extra half-rotation comes from the forward takeoff.
The world record for the heaviest deadlift is 501 kg (1,104.5 lbs), set by Hafþór Björnsson in 2020.
In synchronized diving, both divers must perform identical dives simultaneously — judges score both execution and synchronization.
The Olympic motto, 'Citius, Altius, Fortius — Communiter' (Faster, Higher, Stronger — Together), was updated in 2021.
In professional soccer, the average player runs about 10-13 kilometers (6-8 miles) per match.
The fastest recorded pitch in Major League Baseball is 105.8 mph, thrown by Aroldis Chapman.
In the triple jump, athletes perform a hop, step, and jump in sequence — the world record is 18.29 meters (nearly 60 feet).
Marie-José Pérec of France won gold in the 400m at two consecutive Olympics (1992 and 1996).
In professional American football, the end zone is 10 yards deep.
The world record for the most career hat tricks in soccer belongs to Cristiano Ronaldo.
In competitive sailing, foiling catamarans in the America's Cup can reach speeds exceeding 50 knots (57 mph).
A marathon swimmer at the Olympics races 10 kilometers in open water, typically taking around two hours.

The NBA three-point line was introduced for the 1979-80 season.

Kipchoge's sub-two-hour marathon required pacemakers in a rotating V-formation to minimize wind resistance.
